questions.promptTex: \text{The standard error of the sample mean is:}
questions.explanation: Standard error of the sample mean: $\text{SE} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$.
steps: Standard error measures how much sample means vary across samples.
Larger $n$ → smaller SE → more precise estimates.

questions.promptTex: \text{If you double the sample size, the standard error:}
\text{The Central Limit Theorem says: for } \textbf{large } n, \\ \text{the sampling distribution of } \bar{x} \text{ is approximately:}
hints: CLT: sampling distributions of means → normal.
Even if the population is not normal!
questions.explanation: CLT: for sufficiently large $n$, the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ is approximately **normal**, regardless of the population's shape.
steps: **Central Limit Theorem (CLT):** for large $n$, $\bar{x}$ is approximately normal.
Striking property: this works **even if the population is highly non-normal** (skewed, bimodal, etc.).
The normal distribution emerges naturally from averaging.

questions.promptTex: \text{Standard rule of thumb: CLT applies well when sample size is at least:}
questions.explanation: Common rule: $n \geq 30$ usually suffices for the CLT's normal approximation to be good.
steps: (For very skewed populations, you might want larger; for already-normal populations, much smaller works.)

questions.promptTex: \text{To halve the standard error, you must increase the sample size by a factor of:}
hints: SE goes as $1/\sqrt{n}$. Halving SE means doubling $\sqrt{n}$, so quadrupling $n$.
steps: To make new SE = old SE / 2, need $\sqrt{n_{\text{new}}} = 2\sqrt{n_{\text{old}}}$, i.e., $n_{\text{new}} = 4 n_{\text{old}}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{The mean of the sampling distribution of } \bar{x} \text{ is:}
hints: The sample mean is an **unbiased** estimator of the population mean.
questions.explanation: $E[\bar{x}] = \mu$. The sample mean is centered on the population mean — that's the meaning of 'unbiased estimator'.
steps: Across many samples, the sample means cluster around the **population mean** $\mu$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Population: } \mu = 50, \sigma = 10. \text{ Sample } n = 25. \\ P(\bar{x} > 53) = ? \\ \text{Use the normal approximation; round to 3 dec.}
steps: About **6.7%** chance the sample mean exceeds 53.

questions.promptTex: \text{The margin of error in a poll comes from:}
choices: The standard error of the sample statistic
hints: Margin of error = roughly 2 × SE for ~95% confidence.
questions.explanation: Margin of error reflects the standard error of the statistic — capturing typical sample-to-sample variability.
steps: Margin of error = a measure of how much the sample statistic might differ from the true value due to chance.
It's based on the standard error: typically MoE ≈ $2 \times SE$ for 95% confidence.
Bigger sample → smaller SE → smaller margin of error.

questions.promptTex: \text{A poll of } 1000 \text{ adults shows } 45\% \pm 3\% \text{ support a policy.} \\ \text{To shrink the margin to } \pm 1\%, \text{ you'd need approximately:}
hints: Margin scales as $1/\sqrt{n}$. Shrinking by 3× needs 9× sample.
steps: To reduce margin by factor $k$, multiply $n$ by $k^2$.
From ±3% to ±1% is a factor of 3 reduction → $n \to 3^2 \cdot 1000 = 9000$.

questions.promptTex: \text{If you take samples from a population with } \sigma = 12 \text{ and want a standard error of 2,} \\ \text{what sample size do you need?}
